Text:
OSA recovery: the pattern matters.

A patient with known OSA has repeated airway obstruction and hypoxemia after PACU discharge criteria are otherwise nearly met. What is the best next step?

A. Send the patient home after one normal oxygen reading at rest in recovery
B. Escalate monitoring and consider CPAP if obstruction persists
C. Give a sedative so the patient can rest
D. Remove oxygen and reassess the airway in ten minutes

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

A single reassuring number is not the same as a reassuring recovery pattern. The ASA guidance emphasizes continuous pulse oximetry for patients who remain at increased risk and consideration of CPAP or noninvasive positive pressure when frequent or severe obstruction or hypoxemia occurs. The bedside plan still depends on the procedure, opioid exposure, baseline OSA severity, and the local monitoring pathway.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: ASA Practice Guidelines for Perioperative Management of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
https://www.asahq.org/~/media/sites/asahq/files/public/resources/standards-guidelines/practice-guidelines-for-the-perioperative-management-of-patients-with-obstructive-sleep-apnea.pdf

#CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #BoardReview #MACPrep

Text:
Cefazolin redosing is a clock check.

Quick board pearl: surgical prophylaxis is not finished once the first antibiotic is in. For cefazolin, redosing is generally considered when the procedure reaches four hours from the start of the preoperative dose or when there is excessive blood loss. The exact timing should follow the drug, patient factors, and your institutional policy.

What belongs on the intraoperative checklist for a long case?

Answer below 👇




The antibiotic clock, not just the first dose.

Source: ASHP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Antimicrobial Prophylaxis in Surgery
https://www.ashp.org/-/media/assets/policy-guidelines/docs/therapeutic-guidelines/therapeutic-guidelines-antimicrobial-prophylaxis-surgery.ashx?hash=A15B4714417A51A03E5BDCAC150B94EAF899D49B&la=en

#AntibioticProphylaxis #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless anesthesia medication preparation area with a surgical antibiotic timer and IV medication tray.

Text:
The question does not end at the correct letter.

The useful part of a missed question is not the moment you see the right letter. It is the moment you can explain why the other choices lost. One distractor may be unsafe. Another may be true in a different clinical setting. A third may be the right drug at the wrong dose or time. That is the kind of separation board questions ask you to make under pressure.

When you review today, choose one missed item and write one sentence for every option. The goal is not to memorize the key. The goal is to make the next similar case feel familiar.

#BoardReview #CAA #SAA #StudySmarter #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless study desk with a board style question, handwritten rationale notes, and a laptop showing study material.

Text:
Delirium prevention starts before emergence.

An older adult is at high risk for postoperative delirium. Which perioperative habit best belongs in the prevention plan?

A. Treat confusion as an expected effect of anesthesia
B. Use a structured prevention bundle across recovery
C. Give benzodiazepines at the first sign of agitation
D. Wait for severe delirium before changing the room

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

Delirium prevention is not one medication or one PACU conversation. Multicomponent, nonpharmacologic prevention programs are the most consistently supported approach. The anesthetic plan is one part of a broader perioperative team effort that also includes avoiding deliriogenic medications when feasible and helping the patient reorient after surgery.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: American Geriatrics Society postoperative delirium guideline
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5901697/

#PerioperativeCare #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A quiet, faceless postoperative care environment with orientation aids, glasses, and a recovery chair prepared for an older adult.

Text:
Tourniquet release changes the room.

A tourniquet release is a predictable physiologic transition, not a surprise. After a prolonged lower extremity tourniquet, expect a transient influx of carbon dioxide and metabolites with changes in hemodynamics. The patient’s reserve, tourniquet time, ventilation, and surgical context determine how visible those changes become.

What should you do before release?

Answer below 👇




Look at the patient, the monitor trend, ventilation, and team timing before the clamp comes down.

Source: Tourniquet application during anesthesia: What we need to know
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5187604/

#OrthopedicAnesthesia #CAA #SAA #ClinicalPearl #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless orthopedic surgery setup with a limb tourniquet, anesthesia monitor, and prepared ventilation equipment.

Text:
Keep an error log that asks for a decision.

An error log should be more than a graveyard for missed questions. Give each miss a short label: knowledge gap, stem reading error, timing error, or changed mind without a reason. Then write the decision rule you want to remember next time. For example: when the waveform and clinical story conflict, verify the measurement system before treating the number.

That one sentence turns a vague memory into something you can use on the next set. Review the log weekly and let it choose the next ten questions for you.

#StudyHabits #CAA #SAA #BoardReview #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless study workspace with a notebook labeled by topic, colored tabs, and a focused board review session.

Text:
BCIS: notice the timing.

During cementation in hip arthroplasty, oxygen saturation and end tidal carbon dioxide fall while blood pressure drops abruptly. What is the most useful first interpretation?

A. Assume this timing is unrelated to the current surgical step or event
B. Call possible BCIS and support oxygenation and circulation
C. Wait for a chest radiograph before any intervention
D. Give a sedative and observe for another ten minutes

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

The timing is the clue. Bone cement implantation syndrome may present around cementation, prosthesis insertion, joint reduction, or tourniquet release, with hypoxemia, hypotension, arrhythmia, or cardiovascular collapse. The response is immediate recognition, clear communication, high inspired oxygen, and supportive hemodynamic management tailored to the patient and the local emergency protocol.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: Bone Cement Implantation Syndrome: Key Concepts for Anesthesiologists
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30694541/

#OrthopedicAnesthesia #CAA #SAA #CriticalEvents #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless orthopedic operating room scene with anesthesia monitoring during hip arthroplasty preparation.

Text:
ETT cuff pressure: measure it.

A pilot balloon can feel reassuring and still be misleading. A cuff manometer gives the number. A commonly cited target range is 20 to 30 cm H2O, balancing the need for a seal with the aim of limiting excessive tracheal wall pressure. Recheck when case conditions change, particularly when nitrous oxide, positioning, or airway pressure can change the system.

What is the better habit after intubation?

Answer below 👇




Use a manometer. Feeling the pilot balloon is not the same as knowing the pressure.

Source: Endotracheal Tube Cuff Pressure Assessment study
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32199655/

#AirwayManagement #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless airway management setup with an endotracheal tube cuff manometer and sterile airway supplies.

Text:
Study the blueprint, then study the patient.

It is easy to keep studying the topics that already feel comfortable. It also feels productive. A blueprint guided plan is a little less flattering and far more useful. It asks which clinical areas are weighted, where you are slow, and what you avoid when you build your own quiz. That is where the next block of study time belongs.

Open the content outline before your next session. Pick one domain you have been postponing, then do a short mixed set that forces you to retrieve it alongside familiar topics.

#NCCAA #CAA #SAA #BoardPreparation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless study planning desk with a CAA exam content outline, calendar blocks, and organized clinical notes.

Text:
PONV: build the plan to the patient.

A young adult presents for laparoscopic surgery. She is a nonsmoker with motion sickness, nausea after a prior anesthetic, and likely postoperative opioid use. Which plan best fits her risk?

A. Give one antiemetic as the case is ending
B. Use risk matched multimodal prevention
C. Treat only if nausea develops in recovery
D. Skip prophylaxis because she is otherwise healthy

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

Postoperative nausea and vomiting is not simply a longest drug list problem. Patient risk, procedure, anesthetic choices, and planned analgesia all matter. The consensus guideline supports baseline risk reduction plus multimodal prophylaxis for patients with meaningful risk. The exact agents and doses should follow patient factors and local protocol.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: Fourth Consensus Guidelines for the Management of Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32467512/

#PONV #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless perioperative assessment desk with an antiemetic plan, risk checklist, and recovery monitoring supplies.

Text:
An arterial waveform can be wrong in a useful way.

A clean arterial waveform is data. A distorted one is a warning about the measurement system. An underdamped trace can exaggerate systolic pressure. An overdamped trace can flatten the waveform and underestimate systolic pressure. Before you treat a surprising systolic number, use the fast flush test and inspect the line for the common mechanical causes.

Many oscillations after a fast flush suggest what?

Answer below 👇




An underdamped system, often with systolic overshoot.

Source: Abnormal arterial pressure waveform damping review
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11297833/

#Hemodynamics #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless anesthesia monitor displaying an arterial pressure waveform with a transducer setup and fast flush device.

Text:
Board prep should sound like the work you do.

CAA board preparation lives at the intersection of physiology, pharmacology, clinical judgment, and speed. It should not feel like a generic collection of disconnected facts. A strong question starts with a patient, asks for a decision, and gives you the source and reasoning to understand the choice after you commit to it.

That is the standard we hold MACPrep to: CAA and SAA focused material, direct citations, and a rationale for each option. If a question teaches you only one fact, it is leaving value on the table.

#CAA #SAA #NCCAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless anesthesiologist assistant study scene with an anesthesia workstation, clinical notes, and a focused preparation session.

Text:
SGLT2 medications: ask before the first incision.

A patient uses an SGLT2 inhibitor and is scheduled for elective surgery. What preoperative detail deserves deliberate verification?

A. Verify fasting glucose, then begin the planned routine surgery
B. Verify the medication hold and ketoacidosis risk
C. Verify a morning schedule and fasting duration
D. Verify any prior insulin use before proceeding

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

SGLT2 inhibitors can contribute to perioperative euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis, so a reassuring glucose value does not rule it out. Current guidance commonly calls for holding most SGLT2 inhibitors for at least three days before scheduled surgery, with four days for ertugliflozin. For an urgent case or a missed hold, use the institution’s pathway and evaluate the clinical context rather than relying on glucose alone.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: Perioperative Management of Patients Receiving SGLT2 Inhibitors
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37932195/

#PerioperativeMedicine #CAA #SAA #DiabetesCare #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless preoperative medication reconciliation scene with a clinician reviewing a diabetes medication list.

Text:
Airway fire prevention is a shared setup.

The fire triangle applies in the OR: oxidizer, ignition source, and fuel. In a high risk airway case, prevention is a team conversation before the first incision. Use the lowest feasible oxygen concentration, avoid nitrous oxide when appropriate, communicate before ignition, and make sure the team is ready to act if the conditions change.

What is the best time to discuss airway fire risk?

Answer below 👇




Before ignition, while there is still time to change the setup.

Source: ASA Practice Advisory for the Prevention and Management of Operating Room Fires
https://www.asahq.org/~/media/sites/asahq/files/public/resources/standards-guidelines/practice-advisory-for-the-prevention-and-management-of-operating-room-fires.pdf

#AirwaySafety #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaSafety #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless airway surgery setup with a laser safe endotracheal tube, suction, wet sponges, and anesthesia equipment.

Text:
Use the source as a study tool, not a decoration.

A citation is most useful when it lets you ask a better follow up question. Is this a consensus recommendation or a trial result? Does the guideline fit the patient in the stem? What changes the decision? When a rationale points you back to the source, you can build a clinical rule instead of collecting isolated trivia.

You do not need to read every paper cover to cover. Start by reading the exact section that supports the decision, then return to the question and see whether you can explain it in your own words.

#EvidenceBased #CAA #SAA #BoardReview #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless study desk with a clinical guideline open beside a question rationale and highlighted notes.

Text:
TIVA: treat the delivery system as part of the anesthetic.

During TIVA, an infusion line is found disconnected from the patient. What is the immediate learning point?

A. Rely on vital signs alone to confirm adequate anesthetic depth
B. Restore delivery and check the infusion system
C. Continue unchanged because paralysis is present
D. Ignore the line while blood pressure is stable

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

With TIVA, the delivery path is central to the anesthetic plan. A disconnected line, incorrect pump setting, or other equipment problem can create a gap in anesthetic delivery. The ASA advisory emphasizes equipment checks and risk aware planning. The appropriate clinical response depends on the moment in the case, the drugs in use, and the patient, but the reflex is the same: verify delivery before assuming the anesthetic is intact.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: ASA Practice Advisory for Intraoperative Awareness and Brain Function Monitoring
https://www.asahq.org/~/media/sites/asahq/files/public/resources/standards-guidelines/practice-advisory-for-intraoperative-awareness-and-brain-function-monitoring.pdf

#TIVA #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaSafety #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless total intravenous anesthesia setup with organized infusion pumps, labeled tubing, and a patient monitor.

Text:
A block wearing off should not be a surprise.

A peripheral nerve block can be excellent analgesia and still need a handoff plan. Rebound pain is the abrupt increase in pain some patients experience as a block resolves. Set expectations early, build scheduled multimodal analgesia into the discharge plan when appropriate, and explain when to begin it. The best instruction is the one the patient can follow before pain catches up.

When should the rebound pain conversation happen?

Answer below 👇




Before discharge, not during the midnight phone call.

Source: Managing rebound pain after regional anesthesia
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7533186/

#RegionalAnesthesia #CAA #SAA #AcutePain #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless regional anesthesia discharge planning scene with an arm sling, written medication schedule, and ice pack.

Text:
Practice and review are different jobs.

Practice tests retrieval, pacing, and commitment. Review changes your next decision. If every study session feels like taking more questions, you may be measuring the same gap repeatedly without closing it. If every session is review, you may feel comfortable without testing whether you can pull the answer back under time pressure.

Try a simple split: answer a short mixed set without notes, then spend at least as long reviewing the misses and close calls. The second half is where the score improvement usually starts.

#StudyStrategy #CAA #SAA #BoardPreparation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless split study workspace with a timed question set on one side and a detailed review notebook on the other.

Text:
Urgent DOAC cases: start with the clock.

An urgent operation is requested for a patient taking apixaban. Which information most directly frames the next conversation?

A. Old INR, incision time, blood type, and oxygen saturation
B. Last dose, renal function, procedure risk, urgency, and indication
C. Room assignment, blood pressure, fasting duration, and surgeon preference
D. Bottle details, refill timing, glucose, vital signs, and a prior INR

Drop your answer below 👇




Answer: B.

Urgent DOAC management is a structured risk assessment, not a single laboratory value. The time of the last dose, renal function, procedure bleeding risk, clinical urgency, and thrombotic indication guide whether delay, measurement, reversal, or another strategy is appropriate. Coordinate early with surgery, anesthesia, pharmacy, and any relevant specialists, using the local protocol for reversal and laboratory testing.

Save this for your next review block. Then open the source and make the rule your own.

Source: AHA Scientific Statement on Reversal of Direct Oral Anticoagulants
https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001285

#PerioperativeMedicine #CAA #SAA #AnesthesiaEducation #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless preoperative planning desk with a clock, anticoagulant medication list, and coordinated surgical plan.

Text:
Low pressure alarm: work from patient to machine.

A low pressure alarm is not a diagnosis. It is a prompt to verify ventilation and find the leak or disconnection. Start with the patient and the circuit you can see, then work through the endotracheal tube or supraglottic device, connections, sampling line, breathing circuit, and machine. If needed, hand ventilate with a self inflating bag while the system is checked.

What is the first priority?

Answer below 👇




Confirm the patient is being ventilated before troubleshooting the equipment in detail.

Source: Association of Anaesthetists monitoring recommendations
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5063182/

#AnesthesiaMachine #CAA #SAA #PatientSafety #MACPrep
Alt text(s) - Separated by ||: A faceless anesthesia machine and breathing circuit with a low pressure alarm, self inflating bag, and airway equipment.

Text:
A Sunday plan that fits real life.

You do not need a perfect six hour study block to keep moving. A realistic Sunday plan can be ten mixed questions, one missed topic from the week, and one source you want to understand better. Decide the time before the week begins. Choose the topic now. Leave the materials open and ready so the first step on Monday is small.

Consistency wins because it keeps the decisions easy. What are your next ten questions going to teach you?

#SundayStudy #CAA #SAA #BoardReview #MACPrep
